title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita, 2024

Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita energy derived from fossil fuels, measured in kilowatt-hours on a logarithmic horizontal axis, and per capita carbon dioxide emissions, measured in tonnes on a logarithmic vertical axis. Each circle represents a country, with size corresponding to population and color denoting the latest income group classification. The data points form a tight, positive diagonal band rising from the bottom left to the top right, showing that higher fossil fuel energy consumption coincides with higher carbon dioxide emissions. At the lower end of the scale, Pakistan and the Philippines, classified as lower-middle-income countries, sit below 5,000 kWh and under 1 tonne of emissions. Moving toward the middle, upper-middle-income nations like China and Mexico cluster between 20,000 and 30,000 kWh, with emissions ranging from roughly 3 to 8 tonnes. High-income countries dominate the upper right quadrant. The United States, South Korea, and the Netherlands appear between 50,000 and 70,000 kWh, with emissions generally falling between 10 and 15 tonnes. Qatar and Kuwait occupy the extreme upper right, exceeding 100,000 kWh and 20 tonnes of emissions. Large circles for India and China indicate their massive populations relative to smaller nations like Ireland or Singapore. The chart notes that land-use change emissions are excluded from these figures. The visual arrangement confirms a direct association where increased per capita fossil fuel energy use aligns with increased per capita carbon output across all income levels.
